topic: Section 1.9 — Differential Refraction: The Eclipse Geometry Trap (post-EXP-162 integration review)
our_argument_summary: Section 1.9 (post-EXP-162) now (a) concedes the single-observatory case (WIN-010) explicitly, (b) anchors the differential-refraction critique onto multi-station and ensemble scaling WINs (WIN-025, WIN-044, WIN-068) plus WIN-039 lunar-distance coupling, (c) reframes the radial-only structure of n(r) as strengthening (not weakening) the shadow-path argument, (d) replaces the directionally-unconstrained angular-diameter speculation with an over-determined historical constraint (durations match to milliseconds, ratio ≈ 1.0 across millennia of eclipses), (e) issues a binary public falsification challenge tied to the August 12, 2026 eclipse, and (f) softens the TLDR to remove the contested 'physical overlap vs optical coincidence' framing.

data_sources_used: Dome model page n(r) / H(r) / sun / moon altitudes
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Unchanged from SEC-1.9.c1 — dome model page still lists no OPEN problem for moon-medium interaction; no published FSF derivation accounting for n(r).
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None introduced by EXP-162. Section's numerical invocations (n=1.44 equator, n=2.84 ice wall, moon at r≈10,000 km, sun gap ≥1,500 km) match the dome's published H(r) and inferred n(r). See holes below for ONE numerical framing issue with the Aug 2026 ray-path n range.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section-level review — no per-WIN monitoring tag applies.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Argument remains original (differential refraction → multi-station scaling incomputable) and is not a relabeled standard mechanism.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Structural argument built from first-principles Snell-law reasoning; no post-hoc observation fit.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: All numerical claims (n range, r ranges, moon-altitude inference, sun gap) derive from the dome's published H(r) and our Section 1.5 inferred n(r).

holes_found.description: AUG 2026 n-RANGE OVERSTATED IN THE NEW P3 PARAGRAPH. New paragraph 'The shadow path is horizontal, not vertical' says: 'Along the August 2026 ground track the ray between the sun's disc position and an INTERMAGNET observatory crosses n values ranging from near 1.0 over the pole-proximate region to 1.44 at the equatorial ring.' Verification: the August 12, 2026 total eclipse ground track crosses Greenland (~80°N → r≈1,112 km, n≈1.028), Iceland (~65°N → r≈2,780 km, n≈1.076), Spain/Portugal (~40°N → r≈5,560 km, n≈1.181). The sub-solar point during eclipse totality is near lat ≈14.5°N → r≈8,340 km, n≈1.326. A straight ray from the sun's disc position to any INTERMAGNET observatory on this track traverses n values in the range ≈1.03 to ≈1.33, NOT 'to 1.44 at the equatorial ring.' The equatorial ring (r=10,008 km) lies SOUTH of the entire Aug 2026 track — the ray doesn't cross it. 'To 1.44' is true of the dome disc's full n range, but it is not true of the Aug 2026 eclipse ray path. A careful dome defender can point at a globe, show that the Aug 2026 track stays between ~40°N and ~80°N, compute n(r) along that span with our own formula, and say 'your own prose describes a ray path with at most a 1.3-fold refractive variation — a factor well within pedestrian atmospheric-refraction corrections used by every professional observatory.' The argument for a large differential effect survives qualitatively, but the quantitative claim is off by about Δn ≈ 0.1.
holes_found.recommendation: Reword: either (a) drop the 'Aug 2026 ground track' scoping and make a generic statement about disc n range, OR (b) keep the Aug 2026 framing but use the honest numbers: 'the ray from the sun's sub-solar position (r ≈ 8,340 km, n ≈ 1.33) to an INTERMAGNET observatory at mid-to-high latitude crosses n values ranging from ≈ 1.03 to ≈ 1.33, a ~30% refractive variation across the path.' Option (b) is still damning for per-station scaling but no longer vulnerable to a 'your own numbers don't match' counter-attack.

holes_found.description: EMPTY CLASS B IS RHETORICAL PADDING. The new 'What the critique does and does not claim' paragraph enumerates three classes of eclipse predictions: (i) single-observatory, (ii) per-station scaling, (iii) eclipse path and totality predictions. Classes (i) and (ii) name specific WINs. Class (iii) is described in the prose ('apparent paths, not physical paths, whenever n(r) is on') but EXP-162's own classification table lists `"wins": []` for class B — empty. A dome defender reading carefully will note the taxonomy is pedagogically tidy but structurally carries only two classes with actual WINs. This weakens the 'comprehensive coverage' framing. Worse, the prose makes an assertion about eclipse path / totality predictions ('the dome has never reconciled the two') without tying it to any WIN or published dome claim — it floats. If no dome WIN currently predicts eclipse paths or totality durations, the claim is true but vacuous. If some WIN does, we should name it.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) collapse classes (ii) and (iii) into one class ('Any prediction consuming derived shadow geometry — per-station scaling or eclipse path/totality'), OR (b) name a WIN or a dome website claim that fits class (iii). Candidates: the dome's eclipse-path publications for Aug 2026, or prediction PRED-095 if it includes totality-duration claims. Without a named target, class (iii) should be cut or merged to avoid the 'three-class taxonomy with one empty class' critique.

holes_found.description: FSF-INPUT CLAIM IS ASSERTED WITHOUT A QUOTED DEFINITION. P2 says: 'the scaling formula itself consumes shadow geometry, not magnitudes.' This is the load-bearing sentence — it's what makes the apparent-magnitude-sufficiency defense fail for multi-station WINs. But our review has never published a direct quote of the FSF formula's inputs from the dome's own source material. If a defender produces an FSF implementation that takes per-station eclipse magnitude (observationally fixed) as input — with amplitude = κ × magnitude^α or similar — the entire paragraph collapses. The dome's published FSF (per WIN-044 claim) 'derives from Version 12 geometry,' which implies geometric inputs, but we don't have a direct citation pinning the specific inputs.
holes_found.recommendation: Before the Aug 2026 test, the decider or analyst should pull the actual FSF definition from the dome's repo (raw-text/monitor.py or the model page) and quote it verbatim in Section 1.9 or a footnote, showing the geometric inputs. If the FSF turns out to take magnitudes as input, the differential-refraction argument needs to pivot to 'then FSF isn't a dome-geometry derivation' — which is already WIN-044's Self-Contradicted verdict, but the pivot should be made explicit. This is also the August-2026 falsification path: if the dome publishes an FSF with bent-ray geometry, we note the new derivation; if they publish straight-line, they contradict Section 1.8's coordinate collapse; if they publish magnitude-based, they concede FSF is not a dome-native derivation.

holes_found.description:
HTML-HYGIENE: TWO MISSING

TAGS. The integration dropped two paragraph-closing tags. The 'Why this matters for eclipses' paragraph ends with 'The dome has never computed this bent shadow path.

What the critique does and does not claim.' — no

before the new

. Same pattern after the 'Preemptive response' paragraph: '...it is a contradiction.

The shadow path is horizontal...' Count confirms: 13

opens, 11

closes — off by 2. Modern browsers auto-close, so there is no visible breakage, but the CSS class styling of the PRIOR paragraph may bleed into the NEW paragraph because HTML5's permissive parsing treats the new

as implicit close. In a future PDF render or stricter HTML linter this may surface as a warning.
holes_found.recommendation:
Simple fix: insert

before both new

paragraphs. This is a 2-character edit in sections.json (part1.html). Flag for structure-integrity agent's next pass if the decider doesn't patch directly.

holes_found.description: ANGULAR-DIAMETER P4 CONFLATES TWO DIFFERENT 'MILLISECOND' FACTS. New P4: 'the sun and moon have agreed in apparent angular size closely enough that both total and annular eclipses are routinely observed, with totality durations matching predictions to milliseconds across millennia (Espenak & Meeus Five Millennium Canon).' The 'to milliseconds' claim is about orbital-mechanics timing accuracy — NASA's Five Millennium Canon predicts eclipse start/end times to milliseconds because lunar orbit parameters and Earth rotation are known to microradian/microsecond precision. This is not, strictly, a constraint on the apparent sun/moon angular-diameter ratio; it's a constraint on WHEN eclipses happen. The ratio constraint comes from the observed existence of both total AND annular eclipses over narrow apparent-diameter-ratio ranges (0.97-1.03). A careful reader may hear 'totality durations matching to milliseconds' and think it's about the ratio, which it isn't. Minor because the overall paragraph still constrains n(r) — via 'must preserve that ratio across every observed eclipse geometry' — and the timing point is independent supporting evidence, not the load-bearing claim. But the reader is briefly misled about what the milliseconds fact is evidence FOR.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider rewording to separate the two constraints: 'the sun and moon have agreed in apparent angular size closely enough that total and annular eclipses alternate across geometry (0.97-1.03 apparent-diameter ratio range), AND eclipse start/end timing independently matches orbital-mechanics predictions to milliseconds across millennia.' Current phrasing is not factually wrong but is rhetorically entangled.

holes_found.description: SECTION 1.5 WAS FIXED, BUT PART 2 STILL CONTAINS THE EQUATOR-AT-14,105 / n=1.83 TEXT. Cross-section verification: Section 1.5 (part1, at index 16884) now correctly says 'at the equator (r ≈ 10,008 km), n ≈ 1.44' — the legacy stale value has been removed from Section 1.5 per the c1 follow-up. HOWEVER, Part 2 (the Self-Contradictions tab) still contains: 'At the equator (r = 14,105 km, H = 1,662 km), the formula gives n = 1.83, but the table lists n = 1.40 — a 31% discrepancy.' This is in a Part 2 subsection that is discussing the internal inconsistency between the dome's own Height Table and its n(r) formula. The Part 2 usage of 'equator' refers to the dome's equatorial ring at r=14,105 km (introduced by WIN-057 / V12 coordinate system), which is a DIFFERENT concept from the Polaris-derived geometric equator at r≈10,008 km used in Section 1.5/1.8/1.9. A defender could still exploit the semantic ambiguity: 'Your review uses two different r-values for the word equator across Parts 1 and 2. If your own review can't agree on where the equator is on the dome map, why should we trust your refraction math?' This is narrower than the c1 hole #1 but survives.
holes_found.recommendation: File a new follow-up issue (or extend the existing one) to disambiguate 'equator' vs 'equatorial ring' in Part 2 prose. Either consistently use 'equatorial ring (r=14,105 km)' when referring to WIN-057's ring, or add a footnote in Section 1.5 acknowledging the two concepts. Outside EXP-162 scope — this is an inherited inconsistency, not something the EXP-162 integration caused.

holes_found.description: THE WIN-010 CONCESSION DOESN'T FLAG A SUBTLER CIRCULARITY. P2 concedes single-observatory cases escape the differential-refraction objection because 'apparent eclipse magnitude at a single point is a catalog-readable number.' True — but NASA's eclipse-magnitude catalogs are themselves computed under globe geometry with atmospheric-only refraction. If the dome uses NASA's predicted magnitude AS input to its WIN-010 prediction, the dome is implicitly using globe-geometry-derived input — which is a separate form of self-contradiction independent of n(r). We don't deploy this. Severity minor because WIN-010's Std-Model-Explains verdict already handles it (Chapman 1933), but it's an argument we could have made and chose not to.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional augmentation to WIN-010's detail_extra or to Section 1.9's concession paragraph: 'Even where apparent-magnitude inputs escape the differential-refraction critique, they rely on NASA catalogs computed under globe geometry — the dome inherits globe-model outputs as inputs without acknowledging the dependency.' Not required for the section to stand; could be a stronger argument on the WIN-010 panel itself.

holes_found.description: 'RADIAL-ONLY n(r)' RHETORICAL FLIP IS SOUND BUT UNDERSPECIFIED. P3 reframes the radial-only ansatz from a defender weapon (hole #3 of c1) into a strengthener: 'A purely radial n(r) bends light most precisely where the ray traverses a wide horizontal sweep of n values.' This is directionally correct — Fermat's principle in a horizontal gradient produces horizontal ray bending, and the shadow path is horizontal across the disc. But the prose doesn't QUANTIFY the bending. A defender could say: 'How much does the ray actually bend? You've asserted it bends but shown no numbers.' The bending angle is approximately (∂n/∂r) × L, where L is path length. For the Aug 2026 ray (L ≈ 5,000-8,000 km across the n=1.03-1.33 gradient), typical ∂n/∂r ≈ (1.33-1.03)/(8340-1112) = 4.2×10^-5 per km. Bending ≈ 4.2×10^-5 × L × integrand ≈ ~0.2 radians = ~11°. That IS a large effect, but we haven't shown it. Deploying the number would harden the paragraph dramatically.
holes_found.recommendation: Compute the expected horizontal ray-bending angle through the Aug 2026 ray path (Fermat / eikonal approximation) and add one sentence: 'For the Aug 2026 ray path, the accumulated horizontal bending is approximately [X] degrees — more than enough to move the ground-track intersection by thousands of km.' This converts the structural argument into a quantitative prediction the dome would need to refute.

stronger_arguments.description: FSF-INPUT PIN: Before Aug 12, 2026, publish a direct quote of the FSF formula's inputs from the dome's own source material (raw-text/monitor.py or model page). This locks down the 'scaling formula consumes shadow geometry, not magnitudes' load-bearing claim. If the FSF turns out to take magnitudes as input, we pivot to 'then FSF isn't a dome-geometry derivation' (already WIN-044 Self-Contradicted); if it takes geometry, the differential-refraction argument bites as stated. Either way, the Aug 2026 test becomes a structural falsifier, not just an observational one.
sources: raw-text/monitor.py (search for FSF / scaling function definitions)
dome model page (look for FSF explicit inputs)

stronger_arguments.description: QUANTITATIVE BENDING ESTIMATE: Compute the horizontal ray-bending through the dome's own n(r) gradient along the Aug 2026 ray path. An order-of-magnitude eikonal estimate gives ~0.1-0.3 radians (~5-15°) across a 5,000-8,000 km ray path — enormous compared to Earth's atmospheric refraction (~0.5° at the horizon). This converts the section's qualitative argument into a numerical prediction that can be compared against any FSF the dome publishes for the 2026 test. Suggested insertion: a sentence in P3 with the bending estimate.
sources: Fermat's principle / eikonal approximation with n(r) gradient
standard atmospheric refraction references for calibration

stronger_arguments.description: NASA-CATALOG INHERITED-INPUT CRITIQUE: Even the conceded class-C single-observatory WINs (WIN-010) rely on NASA-catalog eclipse magnitudes, which are computed under globe geometry. The dome uses globe-model outputs as its prediction inputs without disclosing the dependency. This isn't the differential-refraction critique, but it's a cleaner structural point the dome cannot easily escape — and it applies whether or not n(r) exists.

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-162's central move — conceding the single-observatory case while sharpening the multi-station scaling critique — is adversarially honest and structurally smart. The pre-EXP-162 section overreached by lumping all eclipse WINs into one 'geometry the model cannot compute' bucket; the post-EXP-162 section correctly distinguishes three classes of predictions and takes the hit on the one class where the argument doesn't land. This is the credibility move hole #2 of the c1 review explicitly asked for. The radial-n(r) reframe (P3) is also clever — turning a defender weapon into a strengthener. The angular-diameter reframe (P4) is a strict improvement (from 'might shrink → annular only' speculation to a genuine historical constraint). The Aug 2026 falsification challenge is a genuine public-test move — even if the dome never answers, the challenge on record creates an asymmetric cost for silence.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: N/A — the patches improve the section. The residual holes (n-range overstatement in Aug 2026, empty class B, un-quoted FSF, HTML hygiene, minor framings) are correctable without re-opening the verdict question. None of them undermine the core claim that multi-station and ensemble eclipse WINs rest on shadow geometry the dome has not reconciled with its own n(r).

advocate_mode.best_defense: After EXP-162, the dome has two residual moves: (1) 'Our FSF is computed with Earth-model / globe-standard station coordinates and atmospheric-only refraction — n(r) is a heuristic for describing certain apparent sun positions, not a physical medium we literally apply to ray-tracing.' This concedes the dome's n(r) is not physical — which collapses the sun-apparent-altitude rescue in Section 1.8, a separate fatal move. (2) 'n(r) is radial, so for near-overhead sun-moon-observer geometry the vertical bending is small; the horizontal ray effect you mention is real but bounded by the modest n range along Aug 2026 tracks (1.03-1.33, not 1.03-1.44).' This is hole #1 above — the dome defender can land a small quantitative correction, but the structural argument still holds.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: EXP-162 reduced the defense-survives score from 4 (c1) to 2 (c2). The two residual defenses both have costs: (1) concedes n(r) is not physical (breaking Section 1.8), (2) is a quantitative nudge that doesn't change the verdict. To push from 2 to 1, the decider should fix hole #1 (Aug 2026 n-range) and hole #3 (quote the FSF formula). The August 2026 eclipse observation is itself the falsification vehicle — no further section edits can substitute for the actual test.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No NEW cross-WIN inconsistencies introduced by EXP-162. Verdict-impact per EXP-162 self-assessment: None. Spot-checked: WIN-010 verdict = Std Model Explains (unchanged, section concedes single-obs case); WIN-025 verdict = Std Model Explains (unchanged, section argues additional self-contradiction basis but doesn't recommend verdict change); WIN-039 verdict = Std Model Explains (unchanged, section adds lunar-distance-coupling as another self-contradiction overlay); WIN-044 verdict = Self-Contradicted (unchanged, FSF already self-contradicted on internal-derivation grounds — section adds refraction-based independent contradiction); WIN-068 verdict = Std Model Explains (unchanged, 39-eclipse ensemble). All verdicts remain as-is. One framing note: section says WIN-068 is '39-eclipse latitude-dependent ensemble' — wins.json describes it as '207 geomagnetic manifestations from 100 INTERMAGNET observatories across 39 solar eclipses (1991-2016).' The '39-eclipse' framing is accurate but elides the 100-observatory breadth. Not a hole; just noting.

claims_checked: Sec 1.9 post-EXP162 claim: 'n ≈ 1.03 near the pole' (new bottom line)
Sec 1.9 post-EXP162 claim: 'n ≈ 1.44 at the equatorial ring' (existing and repeated)
Sec 1.9 post-EXP162 claim: 'n = 2.84 at the ice wall' (existing)
Sec 1.9 post-EXP162 claim: 'Aug 2026 ground track ray crosses n values from near 1.0 to 1.44 at the equatorial ring' (NEW — P3)
Sec 1.9 post-EXP162 claim: 'totality durations matching predictions to milliseconds' (NEW — P4)
HTML section_html_length increased from 6,481 (c1) to 10,670 (post-EXP162) — +4,189 chars, matches ~5 paragraph additions/replacements
quantitative_verification.computations: H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619); n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(8537/H(r) − 1). H(0) = 8537, n(0) = 1.000. H(1200) = 7430, n(1200) = 1.030 → 'n ≈ 1.03 near the pole' is accurate at r≈1,200 km which is a fair 'near pole' anchor. H(10008) = 2673.4, n(10008) = 1.439 → 1.44 at equator ✓. H(20015) = 838.4, n(20015) = 2.837 → 2.84 ice wall ✓. Aug 2026 ground track (Greenland 80°N → Spain 40°N, sub-solar ~14.5°N): r span 1,112 - 8,340 km, n span 1.028 - 1.326. A STRAIGHT sun-to-observer ray does NOT traverse to r=10,008 km — 'to 1.44' is overstated. See hole #1. Aug 12, 2026 eclipse date: confirmed via NASA eclipse catalog (total solar eclipse, path over Greenland/Iceland/Spain). Totality durations 'to milliseconds': NASA's Canon predicts total-eclipse start/end times to ~0.1-1 second accuracy for modern eclipses; 'milliseconds' is optimistic but defensible for internal-consistency (ephemeris agreement) rather than absolute timing.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: (a) Aug 2026 n-range claim 'to 1.44 at the equatorial ring' — overstated; actual Aug 2026 ray path n-span is ≈1.03-1.33. See hole #1. (b) 'Matching predictions to milliseconds' is slightly optimistic framing but the underlying point holds. See hole #5.

tldr_review.tldr_text: The dome's refraction fix shifts the sun's apparent position but never computes how the same medium shifts the moon. Eclipse magnetic predictions that scale amplitudes across multiple stations — including the FSF formula staked on the August 2026 test — depend on a shadow path the dome's own n(r) bends without correction. Multi-station and ensemble eclipse WINs rest on geometry the model cannot compute.
tldr_review.issues: New TLDR correctly addresses c1 hole #8: the disputed 'physical overlap, not just optical coincidence' framing is gone. Replacement focuses on multi-station scaling + Aug 2026 test — the load-bearing cases. Length 406 chars (3 sentences), within the norm for ps-tldr summaries elsewhere on the site. Factually accurate and non-overreaching.

deeper_analysis:
EXP-162 is a substantive improvement over the pre-integration section. All six c1-derived holes that were in EXP-162's scope (hole #2 apparent-path sufficiency, hole #3 radial-vs-altitudinal n, hole #4 angular-diameter direction, hole #5 'dominates' overreach, hole #6 WIN-039 missing, hole #8 TLDR overstatement) are addressed. The analyst's concession of WIN-010 to class C is exactly the kind of intellectual honesty that hardens the rest of the argument — a defender who accepts the concession has no further leverage on the other five WINs. The radial-to-horizontal reframe in P3 converts what was a defender's best weapon into an argument strengthener. The August 2026 falsification challenge is the first truly prospective hook in our review — it forces the dome into a binary choice before a specific date, and every outcome costs the dome somewhere.

The residual holes are all correctable in ≤1 day of decider work. Hole #1 (Aug 2026 n-range) is the only one that could feed a live dome-defender rebuttal; all others are hygiene / completeness issues. The FSF-input quote (hole #3 / stronger argument #1) is the single highest-leverage unfinished task — locking down the FSF's inputs with a verbatim dome-source quote converts Section 1.9 from 'inference about what the FSF must consume' to 'direct quote showing what the FSF actually consumes,' and removes the last meaningful defender escape. Overall, defense-survives dropped from 4 (c1) to 2 (post-EXP-162). No verdict changes. Recommended action: minor_edit.

citations_failed: 'Aug 2026 ground track ray crosses n values to 1.44 at the equatorial ring' — the Aug 2026 track stays at ~40-80°N on the globe, r ≈ 1,100-5,600 km on the disc, and the sub-solar point reaches r ≈ 8,340 km. The ray does NOT cross r = 10,008 km. See hole #1. Recommend correction to the actual ray-path n range (≈ 1.03-1.33).
citations_unchecked: FSF formula inputs — cited by reference to WIN-044 but no verbatim dome-source quote. See hole #3 / stronger argument #1.
Espenak & Meeus Five Millennium Canon for eclipse-duration precision — cited but not independently verified. Standard reference; low risk.
